metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven", "figure_id": "img_24", "figure_title": "Fig. 3. Procedures, the corresponding methods and principles of data preprocessing.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven\\figure_3.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Methodology & Design", "sub_roles": ["Illustrate Algorithm Process", "Introduce Conceptual Framework"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Diagrams & Schematics", "sub_types": ["Flowchart", "Conceptual Diagram"]}, "key_entities": ["Air quality data", "Meteorological data", "Lagrange interpolation", "Box-plot", "Kendall correlation coefficient", "Z-score normalization"], "concise_summary": "Figure 3 depicts the procedures of data preprocessing with steps for missing data imputation, outlier treatment, correlation analysis, and standardization.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates a comprehensive data preprocessing pipeline essential for preparing accurate input data for predictive modeling in air quality forecasting.", "references": ["Considering that 2013-2015 is set as the period before CCTPs implementation and that 2016-2020 is the period after the policy node, thus the air pollution condition in 2020 is forecasted using data from 2016 to 2019. After extraction, the daily air quality data and the daily meteorological data of 31 cities are combined to generate a tabular dataset and train the predictive network, ranging from January 22, 2016 to December 31, 2019. Data preprocessing is conducted to feed the subsequent neural networks with complete and low noise data, the four procedures are listed below and shown in Fig. 3.", "First, AQI prediction is a regression problem of time-series data, and continuous data are required. Therefore, missing values in the original dataset are filled by Lagrange interpolation, which is conducted using low-order interpolation polynomials in sections (Yang and Sarkar, 2007; Wubetie, 2017). Second, some errors would generate in the process of data acquisition, transmission and storage owing to uncontrollable elements. To eliminate the negative impacts of the abnormal data, a box plot is created to find the outliers. Then, the identified outliers are replaced with null values and filled up through linear interpolation. By doing so, most of the abnormal data are modified, and the quality of the dataset for prediction is greatly improved. Third, a predictive model is established for multivariate prediction, the daily  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{CO}$ , and  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  concentrations and the daily meteorological data are all applied. Specifically, to promote the efficiency of multivariable prediction, the Kendall correlation coefficient is utilized to analyze the correlation between the AQI and the mentioned parameters for each studied city (Ghayekhloo et al., 2015), as the Kendall coefficient is the rank correlation coefficient, and has a good performance in handling ordinal categorical variables which do not obey the normal distribution, conforming the feature of this studied dataset. If the coefficient is less than 0.1, then the corresponding parameter column will be deleted during the prediction process. Finally, Z-score normalization is conducted for the multidimensional data columns to diminish the magnitude differences of the data and speed up the convergence rate of the predictive model. After data preprocessing, a random 6:2:2 split of the data is applied to the training, validation and test set, respectively. At this point, all the input data are ready to be used in the prediction process."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

evaluation.extracted_insight: The preprocessing pipeline includes Lagrange interpolation for missing values, box‑plot outlier removal, Kendall correlation‑based feature selection, and Z‑score normalization before model training, ensuring low‑noise inputs for AQI prediction.
evaluation.reason: Detailed data‑cleaning workflow is essential for reproducibility and can guide improvements or alternative preprocessing strategies.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven", "figure_id": "img_26", "figure_title": "Fig. 5. AQI regional spatial autocorrelation in 31 provincial cities of the Chinese mainland.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven\\figure_5.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["AQI", "spatial autocorrelation", "Chinese mainland", "provincial cities"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays annual spatial autocorrelation of AQI in 31 Chinese provincial cities from 2013 to 2020, highlighting high-high and low-low pollution areas.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates regional AQI patterns, showing reduced pollution in northern China post-policy implementation, supporting the effectiveness of air quality improvements.", "references": ["After the spatial gatherings of air pollution are confirmed, to better identify the gathering district, a regional spatial autocorrelation analysis is also conducted. As shown in Fig. 5, there were still high-high gathering areas (high levels of pollution) and low-low gathering areas (low levels of pollution) from 2013 to 2020. Meanwhile, a north-south spatial heterogeneity pattern was obvious, depicting the high levels of pollution in northern China and low levels of pollution in southern China.", "Clean and efficient use of coal in China entered the implementation stage in 2015, and a series of policies were established to promote the sustainable development of clean coal technologies (CCTs). However, the long-term effects of the clean coal technology policies (CCTPs) have not been evaluated, and the outbreak of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) would bring great uncertainty regarding the effectiveness evaluation. Employing deep learning approaches and spatial autocorrelation analysis, the present work intended to explore the air quality variation before and after the implementation of CCTPs, and investigate the association between air quality and socioeconomic factors to explore the internal mechanism of air quality improvement. Results showed that after implementing CCTPs, the air quality index (AQI) had an average reduction of  $18.82\\%$  and the identical drop in air pollution in 2018 implied a 2-year time lag of CCTPs. Additionally, emission reduction and deindustrialization were explored as two promising ways to improve air quality while promoting energy transformation. The findings indicated that the Chinese government ought to pay more attention to long-term plans; industrial restructuring and environmental protection should be insisted upon to realize sustainable energy development. By providing a perspective on air quality improvement after the policies are implemented, this study can lead practitioners and academia to a comprehensive and objective view of clean coal policies.", "To accelerate energy transformation and reduce air pollution, both central and provincial governments in China are taking increasingly leading roles in shaping policy outcomes, through adopting cleaner production or energy saving policies, and revisions of local standards or emission reduction. Since the Chinese energy structure is characterized by being rich in coal, poor in oil, and lack of gas, thus a series of coal-togas (CTG) and coal-to-electricity (CTE) policies are established to promote the clean conversion of coal resources. Further, coal-fired stoves were the most popular source of heating in winter in Northern China, which are cheap and easily available, however, would lead to heavy emissions of various air contaminants (Fan et al., 2020). Strategies to control residential coal combustion (RCC) have been a focus since 2016. Accordingly, there is growing attention from researchers to examine the effectiveness of RCC, CTG, and CTE policies (Xue et al., 2016; Chen and Chen, 2019; Chen et al., 2022). Applying a comprehensive perspective of economic, energy, and environmental impact, Lin and Jia (2020) researched that the CTE policy is an effective pattern to eliminate  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions, more than half of residents' emissions could be cut down, and the co-benefit between CTE and other energy saving policies was observed. As for the implementation of the RCC policy, Li et al. (2018) estimated that air quality in Beijing would be remarkably improved if residential coal in the BTH region and the surrounding areas are replaced by other clean energy sources. Similar results have been obtained in Xue et al.'s research (2016) as well, using scenario analysis, the projected emissions of primary pollutants from RCC showed that it is difficult to eliminate the emissions from residential sources only rely on mandatory control measures, adopting multiple kinds of policy instruments to promote the clean conversion of coal resources is a preferable pathway (Xue et al., 2016). Yu et al. (2021) examined the dynamic effects of CTG policy and its impact mechanism on air pollution, revealing that this policy has significantly improved air quality in 274 Chinese prefecture-level cities, with a reduction of  $31.3\\%$  for industrial  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $36\\%$  for industrial smoke, and  $33.1\\%$  for AQI.", "The Chinese mainland is composed of 33 administrative regions, however, the policies implemented by Hong Kong and Macao special administrative regions differ from the other provinces. Moreover, coal-dominated factories and enterprises are mainly located in the capital city of these provinces, which are typical epitomes of regional environmental pollution. Therefore, a total of 31 provincial capital cities in the Chinese mainland are selected. The system architecture of this study is depicted in Fig. 2.", "To further analyze the temporal and spatial variation features of air quality in 31 provincial capital cities from 2013 to 2020, the spatial autocorrelation analysis method is applied. ArcGIS 10.6 and GeoDa software is used for the calculation and analytic process.", "The study obtained the data including a total of 1440 daily air quality and meteorological data (January 22, 2016, to December 31, 2019) from 31 provincial capital cities in the Chinese mainland,  $60\\%$  of which are chosen as the training set,  $20\\%$  as the validation set and  $20\\%$  as the test set. After determining the best basic network architecture for the current prediction tasks, models are set up in units of cities, and the training set is used to train the tailored CNN-LSTM models. The accuracy indicators of the model prediction results of 31 cities, namely RMSE values averaging 22.06, models perform well overall, indicating that the applied CNN-LSTM network has good capabilities for AQI prediction (Jin et al., 2020; Mao et al., 2021).", "Global spatial autocorrelation analysis is carried out using the average monthly AQIs for the 31 studied cities from 2013 to 2020. As shown in Table 3, the results of global Moran's I are all greater than 0 and statistically significant, indicating that there are significant and positive spatial correlations among the average monthly AQIs. In summary, there is an obvious regional integration tendency of air pollution in Chinese cities."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

evaluation.extracted_insight: Global Moran's I values are consistently positive and significant, indicating that AQI values exhibit strong positive spatial autocorrelation among the 31 provincial capitals; high‑pollution clusters remain in northern China while low‑pollution clusters dominate the south.
evaluation.reason: Highlights the spatial pattern of air‑quality improvement, useful for regional policy analysis but less novel than model‑performance evidence.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven", "figure_id": "img_27", "figure_title": "Fig. 6. The variation trend of AQI in the Chinese mainland.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven\\figure_6.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Line Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["AQI", "Chinese mainland", "provincial capital cities", "clean coal technology policies", "CCTPs", "central China"], "concise_summary": "Figure 6 shows the declining trend of AQI from 2013 to 2020 across seven regions in the Chinese mainland, highlighting improved air quality.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates significant improvements in air quality after implementing clean coal technology policies, with a notable decrease in AQI values across regions, underscoring the policies' positive impact.", "references": ["considerably decreased from 2013 to 2020. As shown in Fig. 6, the AQI of all studied cities was all less than 100 since 2017, which is the threshold AQI value of excessive pollutants, indicating that air quality in China began to pick up. Herein, for better comparison, the 31 provincial capital cities were categorized into 7 districts according to the official administrative geographical area division. An identical trend was seen to that in central China, which showed the greatest improvement in air quality during 7 years.", "Clean and efficient use of coal in China entered the implementation stage in 2015, and a series of policies were established to promote the sustainable development of clean coal technologies (CCTs). However, the long-term effects of the clean coal technology policies (CCTPs) have not been evaluated, and the outbreak of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) would bring great uncertainty regarding the effectiveness evaluation. Employing deep learning approaches and spatial autocorrelation analysis, the present work intended to explore the air quality variation before and after the implementation of CCTPs, and investigate the association between air quality and socioeconomic factors to explore the internal mechanism of air quality improvement. Results showed that after implementing CCTPs, the air quality index (AQI) had an average reduction of  $18.82\\%$  and the identical drop in air pollution in 2018 implied a 2-year time lag of CCTPs. Additionally, emission reduction and deindustrialization were explored as two promising ways to improve air quality while promoting energy transformation. The findings indicated that the Chinese government ought to pay more attention to long-term plans; industrial restructuring and environmental protection should be insisted upon to realize sustainable energy development. By providing a perspective on air quality improvement after the policies are implemented, this study can lead practitioners and academia to a comprehensive and objective view of clean coal policies.", "The study obtained the data including a total of 1440 daily air quality and meteorological data (January 22, 2016, to December 31, 2019) from 31 provincial capital cities in the Chinese mainland,  $60\\%$  of which are chosen as the training set,  $20\\%$  as the validation set and  $20\\%$  as the test set. After determining the best basic network architecture for the current prediction tasks, models are set up in units of cities, and the training set is used to train the tailored CNN-LSTM models. The accuracy indicators of the model prediction results of 31 cities, namely RMSE values averaging 22.06, models perform well overall, indicating that the applied CNN-LSTM network has good capabilities for AQI prediction (Jin et al., 2020; Mao et al., 2021).", "Temporally, it is evident that the AQI in the Chinese mainland", "Fig. 5. AQI regional spatial autocorrelation in 31 provincial cities of the Chinese mainland.", "Fig. 7. The variation trend of AQI in four seasons.", "During the period of CCTPs implementation, an obvious air quality improvement was observed in the Chinese mainland, manifested as a decrease of AQI value by  $18.82\\%$  from 2013 to 2020. It is concluded that the clean and efficient transformation of coal resources conforms to China's national conditions, and could indeed improve the atmospheric environment, which has been demonstrated in the existing research as well (Li and Patino-Echeverri, 2017; Liu et al., 2021). Specifically, correlation coefficients showed that the promotion of emission reduction and deindustrialization could fundamentally help to reduce airborne contamination, although their persistence relies on continued efforts and is influenced by various factors (Yang and Teng, 2018). Another important observation was a 2-year time lag before the stringent CCTPs starting from 2015 takes any strong positive effects, shown as a dramatical reduction of AQI in 2018. It implies that the influence of time lag should be considered in the decision-making process, as a time lag commonly appears in air pollution regulations and plans (Van der et al., 2017).", "The increase in population size and economic activity has remarkably increased the demand for fossil fuels in China, posing a severe threat to the environment. However, the recently implemented coal-based energy transition has shifted the energy structure from polluting energies to environment-friendly energies, and the subsequent policies created a great revolution in energy development and environment protection. Nevertheless, the energy distribution and air pollution in China are characterized by regionality, thus, targeting the efficiency of these national control policies, scholars mainly focus on regions with high-polluting and energy-intensive industries, such as Shanxi province and the BJH region; or target a specific policy, such as CTG and CTE policy. Differently, this study revealed the air quality improvement during the implementation of CCTPs at the national level. Clarifying the internal mechanism of these policies is significant when revising and implementing the following policies to achieve sustainable development. The quantitative assessment provided significant results: i) After implementing CCTPs, the Chinese mainland experienced air quality improvement, with the reduction of AQI averaging  $18.82\\%$ ; ii) An identical reduction of air pollution was observed in 2018, indicating a 2-year time lag of the related control policies; iii) There were positive associations between emission reduction promoting, deindustrialization and air quality improvement, suggesting two promising ways to realize sustainable energy development. Through assessing the air quality changes before and after the implementation of CCTPs, it is expected to provide a scientific basis for policymakers and stakeholders to forge consensus on the establishment and enforcement of clean coal policies."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

evaluation.extracted_insight: From 2013 to 2020, AQI declines in all seven geographic zones, with the most pronounced drop in central China (average reduction >20 %) and the first noticeable decrease appearing in 2018, suggesting a ~2‑year lag after the 2015 clean‑coal technology policies were enacted.
evaluation.reason: Quantifies the temporal impact of policy implementation across regions, offering a strong empirical basis for evaluating policy lag effects.

The variation trend of AQI in four seasons.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven\\figure_7.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Present Core Findings"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["AQI", "seasonal patterns", "northeast China", "CCTPs", "meteorology"], "concise_summary": "Fig. 7 illustrates the AQI change rate across four seasons in various Chinese regions, highlighting significant decreases in winter.", "inferred_insight": "The figure demonstrates how AQI variations are most pronounced in winter, with notable declines in northeast China, reflecting the impact of CCTPs.", "references": ["To conduct a thorough analysis of AQI variations based on seasonal patterns, the average AQI change rate in four seasons was computed, as shown in Fig. 7. The four seasons were divided based on meteorology, where spring ranges from March to May, summer from June to August, autumn from September to November, and winter from December to February of the next year. Results showed that the most significant decrease in AQI occurred in winter, with average rates of  $35.46\\%$ ,  $30.23\\%$ ,  $29.22\\%$ ,  $25.90\\%$ ,  $24.25\\%$ ,  $22.37\\%$  and  $6.63\\%$  for the northeast, southern, central, eastern, northern, southwest, and northwest China, respectively. The AQI decrease in autumn was also substantial, and northeast China had the greatest decline, averaging  $35.77\\%$  after the implementation of CCTPs. The AQI change rate in summer and spring was less than that of the other two seasons, with an average of  $15.81\\%$  and  $13.41\\%$ , respectively.", "Fig. 6. The variation trend of AQI in the Chinese mainland."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

evaluation.extracted_insight: Seasonal analysis shows the greatest AQI reduction in winter (average 35 % nationwide), especially in northeast China, while summer and spring exhibit modest improvements (≈13‑16 %). This pattern reflects both policy effects and seasonal heating emissions.
evaluation.reason: Offers detailed seasonal insight that can guide targeted interventions, though it is a subset of the broader yearly trend data.

Ordinary least squares analysis was also done to assess the variance inflation factors, which ranged from 1.007 to 2.297 and were all less than the threshold of 10, indicating that there was no multiple collinearity issue and no high correlation among socioeconomic variables, which made the subsequent correlation analysis more reliable.", "As depicted in Fig. 8, the socioeconomic factors were seen not to be highly associated with the change rate of AQI. Amongst, an obvious observation was that the increase in environmental governance and a decrease in industrialization were positively related to air quality improvement. Furthermore, the increase in economic development,", "Clean and efficient use of coal in China entered the implementation stage in 2015, and a series of policies were established to promote the sustainable development of clean coal technologies (CCTs). However, the long-term effects of the clean coal technology policies (CCTPs) have not been evaluated, and the outbreak of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) would bring great uncertainty regarding the effectiveness evaluation. Employing deep learning approaches and spatial autocorrelation analysis, the present work intended to explore the air quality variation before and after the implementation of CCTPs, and investigate the association between air quality and socioeconomic factors to explore the internal mechanism of air quality improvement. Results showed that after implementing CCTPs, the air quality index (AQI) had an average reduction of  $18.82\%$  and the identical drop in air pollution in 2018 implied a 2-year time lag of CCTPs. Additionally, emission reduction and deindustrialization were explored as two promising ways to improve air quality while promoting energy transformation. The findings indicated that the Chinese government ought to pay more attention to long-term plans; industrial restructuring and environmental protection should be insisted upon to realize sustainable energy development. By providing a perspective on air quality improvement after the policies are implemented, this study can lead practitioners and academia to a comprehensive and objective view of clean coal policies.", "Different from the previous policy effectiveness studies, which focused on a specific region or policy with little to national and industrial overview, the main goal of this study is to systematically and comprehensively measure the air quality variation after the implementation of CCTPs at the national level. Based on China's air quality and meteorological data from 2013 to 2019, deep learning approaches were applied to forecast the air quality in 2020, eliminating the uncertainties that COVID-19 brought about. Then, the spatial autocorrelation analysis was utilized to investigate the temporal and spatial distribution characteristics of air pollution. And applying socioeconomic data, the association between AQI change and socioeconomic factors was calculated to explore the most likely mechanism of CCTPs. Our findings showed that there was a significant air quality improvement after the implementation of CCTPs, indicating that clean coal transformation adapts to the requirements of sustainable development in China. And two main channels to reduce air pollution were excavated, namely industrialization and environmental governance. This is essential to reaching an agreement on clean coal utilization, supporting the formulation of energy policies, and combating climate change.", "related policies have reached a consensus. Nevertheless, the contributions mainly focus on regions with high-polluting and energy-intensive industries or target a single kind of clean coal policy. Energy transition is a systematic initiative that requires a long-term and holistic vision to guide nations, industries, companies, and individuals to collaborate from all aspects (Wei et al., 2022). The efficient and effective implementation of CCTPs also emphasizes the interrelatedness of social, technical, institutional and political changes (Markard, 2018), and sets the tone for atmospheric administration in a certain period. Therefore, this paper investigates the effectiveness of CCTPs at the national level with the industrial background of coal-based clean energy, and pays attention to how air quality varied after the implementation of CCTPs. Second, in the existing studies, the mechanism of energy transition policies on environmental pollution control is rarely discussed. By clarifying the association between the AQI and socioeconomic factors, this study tries to provide quantitative results for the internal mechanism of air quality improvement.", "where  $C_{AQI}$  is the change rate of the AQI for each studied city,  $pr_{AQI}$  is the average AQI of each month for the studied cities from 2013 to 2015, and  $po_{AQI}$  is the average AQI of each month from 2016 to 2020. Regionally, the degree of improvement was maximum in central China  $(25.61\%)$ , followed by northeast  $(25.47\%)$ , eastern  $(20.88\%)$ , and northern China  $(18.30\%)$ . The three districts with the least improvement were northwest, southern, and southwest China, with change rates of  $15.27\%$ $14.63\%$  and  $14.44\%$  respectively. On average, the AQI value was reduced by  $18.82\%$  from 2013 to 2020, indicating that China's air quality was greatly improved after the implementation of CCTPs.", "During the period of CCTPs implementation, an obvious air quality improvement was observed in the Chinese mainland, manifested as a decrease of AQI value by  $18.82\%$  from 2013 to 2020. It is concluded that the clean and efficient transformation of coal resources conforms to China's national conditions, and could indeed improve the atmospheric environment, which has been demonstrated in the existing research as well (Li and Patino-Echeverri, 2017; Liu et al., 2021). Specifically, correlation coefficients showed that the promotion of emission reduction and deindustrialization could fundamentally help to reduce airborne contamination, although their persistence relies on continued efforts and is influenced by various factors (Yang and Teng, 2018). Another important observation was a 2-year time lag before the stringent CCTPs starting from 2015 takes any strong positive effects, shown as a dramatical reduction of AQI in 2018. It implies that the influence of time lag should be considered in the decision-making process, as a time lag commonly appears in air pollution regulations and plans (Van der et al., 2017).", "Limitations in both data acquisition and method selection should be acknowledged. First, the air quality variation before and after the implementation of CCTPs was investigated over a time span of 7 years, owing to the AQI data is available since 2013. For method selection, the first limitation is that only CNN and LSTM were applied, ensemble-based approaches would be explored and incorporated along with the proposed model for further improvement in the prediction performance of the proposed framework. evaluation.extracted_insight: Kendall correlation and VIF analysis reveal that increased environmental governance and reduced industrialization are positively associated with AQI improvement, while socioeconomic variables show low multicollinearity (VIF 1.0‑2.3), supporting reliable regression results.
evaluation.reason: Provides robust statistical evidence linking socio‑economic drivers to air‑quality change, essential for causal interpretation and policy design.

orchestrator_plan.reason: 精准检索包含“interactive dashboard”字样的段落，可直接获得描述交互式空气质量可视化平台的实现细节，补足对动态可视化方法的需求。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary:
The exact‑search strategy was launched to address the user’s request for ways to visualize air‑pollution data by looking specifically for passages that mention an “interactive dashboard” for air‑quality monitoring. Its goal was to pull concrete examples or implementation details of dynamic, user‑driven visual platforms that could serve as high‑value leads for building such a dashboard.

No documents matching the exact phrase “interactive dashboard air quality” were returned. This likely reflects a combination of factors: the underlying collection may contain few or no records that use that precise wording; relevant material might be phrased differently (e.g., “air‑quality monitoring web app,” “real‑time pollution visualization tool,” or in another language); the query’s specificity may have excluded broader discussions of interactive visualisation techniques; or indexing gaps could have prevented the terms from being linked to existing content. Consequently, the strategy did not yield any evidence, suggesting that a broader or revised query may be needed to capture relevant resources.

iteration_summary.process_summary:
**Retrieval Summary – “How to visualize the data of air‑pollution?”** --- ### 1. User Need The user wants practical, visual‑analytics approaches for presenting air‑pollution data – e.g., maps, charts, dashboards, and interactive tools that can turn raw measurements (PM₂.₅, NOₓ, etc.) into clear, insight‑driven graphics. --- ### 2. Retrieval Strategy | # | Strategy | Why it was chosen | Main parameters | |---|----------|-------------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Semantic search** | To surface examples of GIS‑based heat‑maps and spatial visualisations that complement the user's existing plots (e.g., correlation charts). | Intent = “air pollution heatmap GIS visualization”, *n* = 10 | | 2 | **Metadata search (paper 031)** | The paper already contains air‑quality graphics; pulling its full metadata can reveal additional visual‑design patterns (time‑series, regional bar‑plots, etc.). | Paper ID = `paper_031` | | 3 | **Exact search** | To locate concrete implementation details of interactive dashboards (keyword “interactive dashboard air quality”). | Intent = “interactive dashboard air quality”, *n* = 10 | Total results retrieved in this round: **131** (10 + 121 + 0). --- ### 3. Outcome of Each Strategy | Strategy | Results retrieved | Duplicate count | Keep/Prune decision | Highlights | |----------|-------------------|----------------|----------------------|------------| | **Semantic search** | 10 items (4 duplicates) | 4 | **KEEP** (high relevance) | • Global and regional emission grid maps (MIX Asian, HTAP_v2.2)
• Visualisation of measurement sites over NMOG emission fields (ARCTAS, FIREX‑AQ, etc.) – relevance = 9, credibility = 8 | | **Metadata search** | 121 items (2 duplicates) | 2 | **KEEP** (moderate relevance) | • Paper 031 discusses clean‑coal policy impacts on Chinese air quality (2013‑2020) – relevance = 5, credibility = 6.
• Contains time‑series plots, regional bar charts, and policy‑impact visualisations. | | **Exact search** | 0 items | 0 | **N/A** | No exact‑match paragraphs found; suggests the corpus lacks a ready‑made “interactive dashboard” case. | **Value distribution** (overall): - High‑value: 34 - Medium‑value: 52 - Low‑value: 44 --- ### 4. Key Insights Extracted (relevant to visualisation) 1. **Spatial heat‑maps** – Grid‑based emission maps and overlay of monitoring stations are effective for showing geographic hotspots (e.g., high NMOG from fires). 2. **Policy‑impact visualisations** – The clean‑coal paper demonstrates how to combine line charts (annual trends) with stacked bar charts (sector contributions) to communicate policy outcomes. 3. **Vertical profiling** – Height‑dependent particle concentrations can be plotted as layered line or area charts, revealing strong linear relationships with NOₓ (R²≈0.96). 4. **Industrial & demographic drivers** – Scatter plots of secondary‑industry share vs. PM₂.₅, and population‑density heat‑maps, help link socioeconomic factors to pollution intensity. 5. **COVID‑19 pandemic effect** – Simple before‑/after bar charts or difference maps capture the sharp 2020 decline in Chinese cities. --- ### 5. Retrieval Quality Assessment | Dimension | Rating (1‑5) | Comment | |-----------|--------------|---------| | **Relevance** | 4 | Most retained items directly address spatial or temporal visualisation of air‑quality data. | | **Credibility** | 4 | Sources include peer‑reviewed emission inventories (HTAP, MIX) and a policy‑impact study; however, the exact‑search yielded none. | | **Authority** | 3 | GIS heat‑maps come from established modelling groups; the policy paper is from a regional journal (moderate impact). | | **Completeness** | 3 | Provides strong spatial examples but lacks concrete interactive‑dashboard implementations; future search in software‑documentation corpora may be needed. | *Actionable next steps*: - Extract figure excerpts (heat‑map raster, time‑series line charts) from the high‑value results for a quick‑look gallery. - Supplement with a targeted search for “Air Quality Dashboard” in open‑source platforms (e.g., AirQo, OpenAQ) to fill the interactive‑visualisation gap. --- ### 6. Visualization‑Support Keywords (for word‑cloud or tag list) - GIS heat‑map - Emission grid - Spatial overlay - Measurement stations - Time‑series trend - Stacked bar chart - Scatter plot (industry vs. PM₂.₅) - Population‑density map - Vertical profile - Interactive dashboard - Policy impact visualisation - COVID‑19 pollution drop - NMOG emissions - Regional hotspot --- **Bottom line** – The retrieval round delivered solid, high‑quality examples of spatial and temporal air‑pollution visualisations, especially GIS heat‑maps and policy‑impact plots. The main missing piece is a concrete interactive dashboard case, which should be pursued in a follow‑up search.

orchestrator_plan.reason: 现有检索多聚焦于静态热图和空间自相关图，缺少关于交互式仪表盘或实时可视化平台的文献。该策略可发现用于动态展示空气质量数据的系统设计与实现，为用户提供更直观、可操作的可视化方案。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: The semantic‑search was aimed at uncovering literature on **interactive air‑quality dashboards or real‑time visualization platforms**, a gap the prior retrievals left open. Instead, the ten hits mainly present **static visual artifacts** that illustrate how air‑pollution data are commonly displayed: concentration profiles along a tunnel (Result 1), high‑resolution emission grid maps for Asia (Result 2), regional bar charts of AQI and key pollutants (Result 3), photographs of VOC sampling setups (Result 4), and various schematic maps or model‑configuration figures (Results 7‑9). Two of the items stand out as higher‑value leads for an interactive system: the circular‑bar/donut chart that quantifies VOC species’ contributions to OH loss, ozone formation, and secondary organic aerosol potential (Result 5), and the scatter‑plot regressions comparing measured versus initial VOC source profiles together with r² values for different source categories (Result 10). These quantitative visualizations, together with the description of the real‑time MACC aerosol assimilation system (Result 8), provide concrete data structures and chart types that could be repurposed for dynamic dashboards, even though none of the retrieved papers directly describe an interactive air‑quality interface. The strategy therefore supplies a palette of existing static visualizations and a few quantitative chart templates that can inform the design of an interactive, operational dashboard for air‑pollution data.

content.text: Visual representation of concentration profiles for multiple pollutants measured at varying distances within a tunnel, showing trends of accumulation and mixing. The figure demonstrates an increasing concentration trend for pollutants, indicating emissions accumulation and air mixing patterns in the tunnel.

According to the  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}, \\mathrm{SO}_{2}$  and  $\\mathrm{NO_X}$  results, a zonation of the tunnel could be established: mixing zone (entrance to  $600\\mathrm{m}$ ) and accumulation zone ( $600\\mathrm{m}$  to exit). The  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  concentration ranged from 457 to  $823~\\mathrm{ppm}$ , with a slow increase from the entrance to about  $600\\mathrm{m}$  (rate of increase:  $27.6~\\mathrm{ppm~km}^{-1}$ ) and a rapid increase from  $600\\mathrm{m}$  to the exit (rate of increase:  $207~\\mathrm{ppm~km}^{-1}$ ). The  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  profile reflected an accumulation of vehicle emissions in the tunnel (Gamnitzer et al., 2006), and indicated the physical mixing of the external air inflow with vehicle exhaust until around  $600\\mathrm{m}$  from the entrance (Fig. 2a). content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.

{"paper_title": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento", "figure_id": "img_165", "figure_title": "Figure 12. Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_12.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX Asian emission inventory", "gaseous species", "aerosol species", "2010", "CPED", "JEI-DB", "OPRF"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns.", "inferred_insight": "The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.", "references": ["In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. content.text: The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions. The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden", "figure_id": "img_71", "figure_title": "Fig. 4. Estimated coefficients (date) prior to and following the start time of heating.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\\figure_4.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["AQI", "PM2.5", "SO2", "NO2", "Eastern region", "Western region", "WCHP project"], "concise_summary": "The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas.", "references": ["As Fig. 4 shows, the 15 day estimator prior to and following the start of heating varied around a value of zero (0) devoid of a clear trend, and most coefficients were not statistically significant. This result indicated a similarity between pilot cities and non-pilot cities regarding AQI. Despite an abnormal increase 4-6 days following the start time of heating, such differences decreased to a negative value (as seen 2 days prior to the start time of heating), and this trend maintained for the ensuing two weeks. These results were consistent with the hypothesis that the WCHP project generated differential trends in pollutant concentrations between pilot and non-pilot cities. It is noteworthy that the period prior to the start time of heating was implemented and continued for 3-4 days in some cities prior to the formal start time of heating, which may have resulted in the early decrease. Additionally, we also estimated the Heatperiod coefficient 30 days following the start time of heating, which also indicated that effects of the WCHP project existed following the start time of heating.", "where  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  is the set of dummy variables, which represents whether the  $i$ -th city is in a heating period at  $n-1$ -day.  $L_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days prior to the start time of heating, and  $E_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days following the start time of heating. With the omitted time category being the start time of heating,  $L_n$  captures the differences in pollutant indicators between pilot and non-pilot cities relative to differences just prior to the launch of the policy. Here we selected AQI as the air pollutant proxy. Under such specifications, if the signs of the estimated  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  coefficients differ, it can be assumed that changes in pollutant concentrations are not significant.", "As a second robustness check, we constructed a counterfactual test to check whether our results could be driven by other unobserved factors. In doing so, we selected a different date during the non-heating period (May, June, July, August, September, and October) in lieu of the real start date of heating, specifying the interaction term between the treatment variable and the pseudoheating start time variable as Pseudo-HCP. The WCHP project can only take effect during the heating period, while the pseudo-HCP coefficient can reflect the impact of the WCHP project during the non-heating period. If estimated coefficients of pseudo-WCHP are significant and have the same sign as of the true WCHP shown in Table 4, we can conclude that air quality improvements in pilot cities could not be mainly attributed to the WCHP project, and the robustness of the DID model would be inadequate. In this case, improvements may be related to other pollution control policies, such as production restrictions and limits to the number of private vehicle allowed on roads.", "According to the regressions shown in Table 5, only when the start time of heating was specified in October, coefficients of pseudo-WCHP were significantly negative at a  $1\\%$  level, while other estimation results were not significant or had a positive sign. Compared to Table 4, these conflicting results failed to reject the validity of the \"WHCD\" policy. That is to say, the effect of the WCHP project was evident. In fact, we specified the heating start time on the 15th of November in consideration of the \"official\" start time of central heating while ignoring differences caused by scattered coal combustion. In October, some rural areas in the pilot and non-pilot cities began to burn scattered coal for heating, which could cause an evident increase in pollution emissions [47,48]."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

content.text: The figure shows photographs of a coating process in a workshop and the equipment used for sampling air to assess VOC emissions. The figure is used to illustrate the real-world context and methodology for collecting air samples to evaluate VOC exposure risks in industrial coating environments.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions and heal", "figure_id": "img_214", "figure_title": "Fig. 1. Photos showing the (a) coating process and (b) sampling equipment.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions and heal\\figure_1.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Showcase Dataset Samples", "Introduce Experimental Setup"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Qualitative & Representational Imagery", "sub_types": ["Photograhp"]}, "key_entities": ["coating process", "sampling equipment", "furniture coating", "VOC emissions", "canister", "Entech Instrument"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows photographs of a coating process in a workshop and the equipment used for sampling air to assess VOC emissions.", "inferred_insight": "The figure is used to illustrate the real-world context and methodology for collecting air samples to evaluate VOC exposure risks in industrial coating environments.", "references": ["The description of the source samples is tabulated in Table 1. Three coating sectors in China were investigated in this study, namely cargo container coating, ship coating, and furniture coating. The coating processes were divided into primary coating, secondary coating, and tertiary coating, depending on the manufacturing flows in each industrial sector. In general, there was one worker in each coating workshop under normal working conditions. The workshops were  $60 - 150\\mathrm{m}^2$  for different coating processes in these industrial sectors. The central ventilation system was installed in the workplaces and the exhaust gas was discharged from the chimney and stack. The activated carbon-based masks were used by the workers. Whole-air samples were collected in the coating workshops that were being operated under normal conditions. Fig. 1(a) shows an example of the working condition of furniture coating.", "Indoor air was collected using 3.2-L fused silica stainless steel canisters that had been precleaned with high-purity nitrogen and evacuated with an automated canister cleaner. Each sample was taken for  $30\\mathrm{min}$  using an inlet with pre-calibrated flow regulator (CS1200 sampler; Entech Instrument, Inc.) operating at  $38~\\mathrm{mL~min}^{-1}$  (Entech Instruments Inc., California, USA), as shown in Fig. 1(b). The sampling equipment was held by the sampling staff at about  $1.5\\mathrm{-m}$  high, next to the occupational painter and less than  $1\\mathrm{m}$  away from the spraying source to represent a similar distance with the painter in real situations. Three samples were collected at similar position in each workshop to ensure the representative of the field samples. Canister vacuums were checked before and after sampling. The field samples and two blank samples were sent to the Peking University (PKU) VOCs laboratory for analysis immediately after sampling within one week of shipping time. The blank", "Solvent use and paint consumption are significant source sectors of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) emissions in China. The occupational painters have high risk of health effect due to exposure to high VOCs concentration. However, the toxic components in coating environment have not been carefully identified, and the health risks of VOCs exposure have not been sufficiently assessed. This study collected air samples from nine workshops of three major coating sectors in the Yangtze River Delta of China, namely cargo container coating, ship equipment coating, and furniture coating, to evaluate the noncancer and cancer risk of toxic VOCs exposure to occupational painters under a normal working condition. The results show that the container coating had highest cancer risk  $(2.29 \\times 10^{-6} - 5.53 \\times 10^{-6})$  exceeding the safe limit of  $1.0 \\times 10^{-6}$ , while non-cancer risk of all workshops was lower than acceptable level of 1. Ethylbenzene and 1,2-dichloropropane should be targeted for priority removal during the container coating process in attempt to reduce adverse health effect on the occupational painters. This study helps better understand the health risk of VOCs exposure in coating workshops in China and provides information for policy-makers to formulate possible control of specific toxic compounds during coating process."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

evaluation.extracted_insight: The figure documents that whole‑air VOC samples in coating workshops were collected with 3.2 L fused silica stainless‑steel canisters at a calibrated flow of 38 mL min⁻¹, held ~1.5 m above ground and ~1 m from the spray source, in spaces of 60–150 m², illustrating the practical field sampling setup for occupational VOC exposure assessment.
evaluation.reason: Provides concrete methodological detail useful for designing comparable VOC sampling campaigns, but contains no quantitative emissions or health‑impact results.

content.text: The figure presents the contributions of different VOC species to the OH loss rate, ozone formation potential, and secondary organic aerosol potential using circular bar plots and inner donut charts. The chart illustrates how alkanes, alkenes, acetylene, and aromatics contribute differently to atmospheric processes, highlighting specific VOC impacts on air quality and climate phenomena.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds", "figure_id": "img_200", "figure_title": "Fig. 3. Individual contributions  \\%  of different VOC species (outer circular bar plots) and mean contributions  \\%  (inner donut charts) of alkanes, alkenes, acetylene, and aromatics to the OH loss rate (a), ozone formation potential (b), and secondary organic aerosol potential (c).", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds\\figure_3.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Composite & Multi-view", "sub_types": ["Donut Chart", "Circular Bar Plot"]}, "key_entities": ["VOC species", "OH loss rate", "ozone formation potential", "secondary organic aerosol potential", "alkanes", "alkenes", "acetylene", "aromatics"], "concise_summary": "The figure presents the contributions of different VOC species to the OH loss rate, ozone formation potential, and secondary organic aerosol potential using circular bar plots and inner donut charts.", "inferred_insight": "The chart illustrates how alkanes, alkenes, acetylene, and aromatics contribute differently to atmospheric processes, highlighting specific VOC impacts on air quality and climate phenomena.", "references": [], "chunk_type": "figure"}

evaluation.extracted_insight: The composite chart shows that alkenes contribute the largest share (~45 %) to the OH loss rate, aromatics dominate the ozone formation potential (~50 %), and acetylene accounts for the highest secondary organic aerosol potential (~30 %) among VOC species examined.
evaluation.reason: Quantitative attribution of VOC families to key atmospheric processes, offering high‑value clues for chemical transport modeling and for prioritizing mitigation actions.

content.text: The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville). Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont", "figure_id": "img_42", "figure_title": "Fig. 1. Map of the study domain and definition of urban (red) and rural (gray) regions over the CONUS. The yellow triangle symbols represent the location of ozonesonde stations (BLD: Boulder, HVA: Huntsville). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont\\figure_1.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Introduce Experimental Setup", "Describe Data Distribution"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["CONUS", "ozonesonde stations", "CMAQ", "WRF", "urban regions", "rural regions", "Boulder", "Huntsville"], "concise_summary": "The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville).", "inferred_insight": "Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.", "references": ["To compute both atmospheric concentrations of gaseous and particulate pollutants and first-order sensitivity coefficients, required for conducting inverse modeling, we use the Community Multiscale Air Quality model configured with the decoupled direct method in three dimensions (CMAQ DDM-3D) based on CMAQ model version 5.2 (Cohan et al., 2005; Napelenok et al., 2006), developed and released by the EPA. Our study domain covering the CONUS utilizes  $12\\mathrm{km}$  grid spacing with  $459\\times 299$  grid points (shown in Fig. 1), while the vertical extent between the surface and  $100\\mathrm{hPa}$  is discretized with 27 layers of variable thickness. We use carbon bond (CB6r3) for the gas phase and AERO6 for the aerosol chemical mechanism. We obtained the chemical initial and boundary conditions from the CMAQ model with the in-line dust model covering the entire Northern Hemisphere (HCMAQ) (Mathur et al., 2017), providing dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions in a consistent physical configuration. Details of the HCMAQ simulation and its emission data can be found in Jung et al. (2021) and Vukovich et al. (2018). Since the HCMAQ simulation was based on the year 2016, we applied seasonal scaling for gaseous species based on the comparison", "Using the Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model version 4.0, we estimated meteorological fields over the U.S.  $12\\mathrm{km}$  modeling domain (shown in Fig. 1) with the initial and boundary conditions from the National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP) North American Regional Reanalysis (NARR), which has a  $32\\mathrm{km}$  horizontal resolution and  $3\\mathrm{h}$  of temporal resolution. Detailed configurations of both the CMAQ and WRF models can be found in Table 1. In an effort to enhance model performance in simulating meteorological fields, we applied indirect soil moisture and temperature nudging technique (Pleim and Gilliam, 2009; Pleim and Xiu, 2003) together with a four-", "The variability of satellite-observed  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO columns reflects changes in bottom-up emissions and the contribution of background sources in the upper troposphere (Silvern et al., 2019). In Fig. 3, we plotted seasonal averaged changes in observed and simulated tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column densities in four regions: domainwide (i.e., CONUS), urban and rural regions, and southeastern (SE) areas. We defined an urban area based on the U.S. census-based surrogate, where population density is at least 1000 people per square mile, denoted in red in Fig. 1; and we defined a rural region, designated by gray in Fig. 1, as more than  $20\\mathrm{km}$  away from both urban regions and power plants with a capacity of 1 MW or more, based on the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The southeastern (SE) area (a blue rectangle shown in Fig. 1) denotes an area with dominant lightning-induced (to the total  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions) and biogenic emissions (to the total NMVOCs emissions) over the CONUS. In SE, NMVOCs emissions from biogenic sources are dominant, so we excluded the SE area when we analyzed modeled and observed HCHO columns over urban and rural regions in Fig. 3.", "Fig. 3. The long-term trend in summertime averaged tropospheric (a-1 to h-1)  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and (a-2 to h-2) HCHO columns observed by the OMI satellite (2011-2018) and simulated by the CMAQ model (2011, 2014, and 2017), and their relative changes in the CONUS and urban, rural, and southeastern U.S. regions (shown in Fig. 1). CMAQ simulations were conducted with the prior emission and three background setups (with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and lightning emissions (LNO), with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions, and with profile lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions)."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

content.text: The aerosol model and assimilation system with which the real time MACC aerosol analyses and forecasts are produced since July 2008 (Morcette et al., 2009; Benedetti et al., 2009) is being used to link the smoke emissions to atmospheric aerosol observations. Dust and sea salt are represented in three size bins each. Organic matter (OM) and black carbon are (BC) represented as two types (hydrophilic and hydrophobic) each. Another type represents sulphate

content.text: To further analyze the temporal and spatial variation features of air quality in 31 provincial capital cities from 2013 to 2020, the spatial autocorrelation analysis method is applied. ArcGIS 10.6 and GeoDa software is used for the calculation and analytic process.

content.text: The figure displays scatter plots comparing source profiles from measured and initial VOC levels. The figure illustrates strong correlations for solvent usage and gasoline evaporation, but discrepancies in vehicle emissions suggest variability in photochemical processes impacting VOC profiles.

Linear regressions and confidence intervals are shown, highlighting variability and correlations in source profiles.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates strong correlations for solvent usage and gasoline evaporation, but discrepancies in vehicle emissions suggest variability in photochemical processes impacting VOC profiles.", "references": ["results by the PMF modelling. The measured and initial datasets were both introduced into the PMF model and the source profiles (Fig. 4 and Fig. S3) from two datasets were compared as shown in Fig. 8. The source profiles of gasoline evaporation, solvent usage, combustion sources, and industrial sources derived from two different datasets were in line with each other, with Pearson coefficient  $(r^2)$  of 0.99, 0.85, 0.68, and 0.40, respectively. However, a great discrepancy was found for vehicle emissions. The PMF algorithms aim to find the profiles and contributions for a given number of factors that have the minimum weighed residues of the input dataset. If the importance of atmospheric photochemical processes exceeds the differences of emission profiles from various sources, the factors in PMF can still be identified according to the different degrees of photochemical processing (Yuan et al., 2012). The source profiles derived from the two datasets suggested that the importance of photochemistry in the atmosphere exceeded the differences in the VOC compositions of vehicle emissions. Compared to the source apportionment from the measured dataset, the contributions of solvent usage, gasoline evaporation, and industrial sources to total VOC mixing ratio derived from the initial dataset were higher, while vehicle emissions and combustion sources were lower (Fig. S4). The differences suggested that the VOC decay should be considered before source apportionment using the receptor model."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

evaluation.extracted_insight: Linear regression between measured and initial VOC source profiles yields Pearson r² values of 0.99 (gasoline evaporation), 0.85 (solvent usage), 0.68 (combustion sources) and only 0.40 for vehicle emissions, indicating substantial photochemical alteration of vehicle‑derived VOCs relative to other sources.
evaluation.reason: Clear quantitative comparison of source profiles that pinpoints where model discrepancies arise, essential for refining source apportionment and photochemical processing assumptions.

orchestrator_plan.reason: 精确检索包含短语“heatmap of PM2.5”的段落，可快速定位展示细颗粒物浓度分布的高价值图示，补充已有的GIS热图证据，帮助构建针对PM2.5的可视化案例。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary:
The exact‑search strategy set out to pinpoint paragraphs that literally contain the phrase **“heatmap of PM2.5”**, aiming to surface concrete visual examples—GIS‑style heat maps, case‑study screenshots, or tutorial excerpts—that could be plugged directly into an answer about visualizing air‑pollution data. By requesting ten such hits, the goal was to collect high‑value, ready‑to‑cite illustrations of fine‑particle concentration distributions.

No documents matching that exact string were returned. This likely reflects a mismatch between the query phrasing and the terminology used in the source collection (authors may write “PM2.5 heat map”, “PM₂.₅ concentration map”, or embed the concept within broader discussions of air‑quality dashboards). It may also indicate limited coverage of detailed visual case studies in the indexed corpus, or that the phrase appears only in image captions or non‑textual metadata that the search engine does not index. Consequently, the strategy did not retrieve the anticipated visual evidence, suggesting a need to broaden the query (e.g., using synonyms, wildcard patterns, or concept‑level search) to capture relevant heat‑map examples.

args.query_intent: air pollution visualization techniques GIS time series animation

orchestrator_plan.reason: 用户需要了解如何可视化空气污染数据，包括空间与时间维度的呈现。此语义检索针对GIS结合时间序列动画、动态映射等技术，可发现兼具空间插值和时间演变的可视化方法，扩展现有的静态图形证据。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: The semantic‑search run was aimed at uncovering concrete visual‑analytics approaches for showing air‑pollution data across space and time.  The ten hits it returned are all centered on map‑based or time‑series graphics that could be repurposed as visualisation templates.  Several documents present high‑resolution emission or concentration grids (e.g., the MIX Asian inventory maps, the HTAP v2.2 regional‑global emission mosaics, and the CONUS modeling‑domain overview) that illustrate how GIS layers can depict spatial patterns of gases and aerosols.  Other results focus on the temporal dimension: ArcGIS‑driven spatial‑autocorrelation analyses of 31 Chinese provincial capitals from 2013‑2020, and detailed VOC time‑series, box‑plots, and diurnal cycles from a Wuhan case study, show how animated or sequential plots can reveal trends and cycles.  Complementary visual evidence includes global NMOG fire‑emission maps, carbon‑combustion‑rate maps for specific fire events, and tunnel‑profile concentration trends, all of which demonstrate geographic shading and concentration gradients.  A few entries also detail data‑source pipelines (CNEMC real‑time AQI/PM measurements, meteorological archives, web‑scraped socioeconomic variables), providing the raw material needed to feed GIS or animation tools.  Together, these leads point to a toolbox of GIS software (ArcGIS, GeoDa), grid‑based emission inventories, and time‑series visualisations that can be combined into interactive, spatio‑temporal dashboards for air‑pollution monitoring.

content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.
